source.definition: A device used to measure and display time, typically featuring a dial with hands or a digital display.

explanation.sourceText: The English word 'clock' comes from the Middle English 'clokke', which was borrowed from the Old North French 'cloque', meaning 'bell', originally from the Medieval Latin 'clocca'.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul englezesc „clock” provine din engleza mijlocie 'clokke', împrumutat din franceza veche nordică 'cloque', care înseamnă 'clopot', originar din latina medievală 'clocca'.

target.definition: Dispozitiv pentru măsurarea și afișarea timpului, de obicei cu cadran și ace sau digital.

explanation.sourceText: The Romanian word 'ceas' derives from the Latin 'clāssis', which originally meant 'class' or 'class of ships', but in the Middle Ages was adopted to designate time-measuring instruments.
explanation.targetText: Cuvântul românesc „ceas” provine din latină, din termenul 'clāssis', care inițial însemna 'clasă' sau 'clasă de nave', dar în Evul Mediu a fost preluat pentru a desemna instrumente de măsurare a timpului.

examples.sourceText: The clock shows 3 o'clock in the afternoon.
The alarm clock rings at 7 o'clock.
examples.targetText: Ceasul deșteptător sună la ora șapte.

examples.sourceText: The clock on the wall is very old.
examples.targetText: Ceasul de pe perete este foarte vechi.

learningTips.text: Remember 'ceas' sounds like 'chess'—imagine playing chess while watching the clock to keep track of your time.
The word 'ceas' comes from Latin, just like many English words starting with 'c', such as 'class' or 'clock'.
'Ceas' is a short, simple word like 'clock', both easy to say and related to time.
Think of having a 'ceas' on your wrist helping you 'check' your appointments—the English word 'check' sounds similar and can help you remember 'ceas'.
